title: 6 failing tests: 2 from wrong patch target, 4 from new validate_executable breaking test fixtures
description: Two test classes of failures exist in the working tree. (1) test_apply_osc_to_terminal_write_error and test_apply_osc_to_terminal_flush_error both patch 'term_chameleon.terminal.sequences_for_preset', but that name is never imported at module scope — it is imported inside the function body at line 74 of terminal.py. The attribute does not exist on the module object, so `patch()` raises AttributeError before the test runs. The correct patch target is 'term_chameleon.osc.sequences_for_preset'. (2) test_watch_live_command_defaults_to_whole_screen, test_watch_live_command_supports_iterm_window_region_and_whole_screen, test_install_watch_daemon_cli_dry_run, and test_install_watch_daemon_cli_iterm_window_opt_in all pass non-existent paths ('/tmp/python', 'python') to watch_live_command(). The working-tree version of watch_daemon.py now calls validate_executable() which checks that the path exists on disk, causing InvalidExecutableError. The tests were written before validate_executable was added and were never updated.
fix: For tests/test_terminal.py lines 71 and 88: change patch target from 'term_chameleon.terminal.sequences_for_preset' to 'term_chameleon.osc.sequences_for_preset'. For tests/test_watch_daemon.py lines 15, 24, 26, 29: use sys.executable or a temporary executable created with tmp_path, or mock validate_executable to avoid filesystem checks in unit tests.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified by reading source and running the tests. All 6 claimed tests fail exactly as described.

Root cause 1 (2 tests): src/term_chameleon/terminal.py imports sequences_for_preset INSIDE the function body at line 74 (`from .osc import reset_sequences, sequences_for_preset`), not at module scope. tests/test_terminal.py lines 71 and 88 patch 'term_chameleon.terminal.sequences_for_preset'. Running the test confirms: `AttributeError: <module 'term_chameleon.terminal'...> does not have the attribute 'sequences_for_preset'`, raised before the test body executes. Correct target is term_chameleon.osc.sequences_for_preset.

Root cause 2 (4 tests): src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py watch_live_command (line 117) calls validate_executable(executable), which (lines 93-100) checks expanded.exists(), is_file(), and os.access(X_OK). The 4 tests pass non-existent paths ('/tmp/python', 'python'). Two unit tests raise InvalidExecutableError; the two CLI dry-run tests return exit code 2 with stderr 'Executable path does not exist: /tmp/python' instead of 0.

pytest run produced exactly '6 failed' for the named tests. The proposed fixes (repoint OSC patch to term_chameleon.osc.sequences_for_preset; use sys.executable / tmp_path-created executables / mock validate_executable in watch_daemon tests) are correct and would resolve the failures.

Severity adjusted from CRITICAL to HIGH: this is a test-suite breakage that blocks CI/a green run, not a production correctness or security defect. The shipped validate_executable behavior is intentional and correct; the failures stem from stale tests and a wrong mock target. No user-facing bug, data loss, or security risk.

title: watch_daemon.validate_executable() breaks test suite and conflicts with dry-run purpose
description: watch_live_command() calls validate_executable(executable) unconditionally at line 117 when executable is not None. This validates that the path exists as an executable file on disk. In a --dry-run scenario (install-watch-daemon --dry-run), no file is written and no subprocess is launched, so validating the executable path at call time is premature and prevents legitimate dry-run previews with planned paths. It also breaks four existing tests that use synthetic paths ('/tmp/python', 'python') as representative non-None executables. The validate_executable function does not check for '$(...)' or backtick shell substitutions (only checks ;, |, &, $, backtick as individual characters) — but $(...) is blocked because '$' is in the deny-list, so that aspect is safe.
fix: Move validate_executable() call out of watch_live_command() and into _install_watch_daemon() in cli.py, only when dry_run=False. Alternatively, skip filesystem checks when the caller has indicated a dry-run context. Update the four broken tests to either mock validate_executable or use sys.executable.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified directly against the code and by running the test suite.

Code confirmation (src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py):
- Lines 114-117: `watch_live_command()` computes `resolved_executable = executable or sys.executable`, then `if executable is not None: validate_executable(executable)`. This is unconditional whenever a non-None executable is passed.
- Lines 74-100: `validate_executable()` performs real filesystem checks — `expanded.exists()` (line 93), `expanded.is_file()` (line 96), and `os.access(expanded, os.X_OK)` (line 99) — raising InvalidExecutableError otherwise.
- These are uncommitted changes (git diff confirms `validate_executable` and the guard were just added; watch_daemon.py shows as modified, not committed).

Dry-run conflict confirmed: In cli.py `_install_watch_daemon()` (lines 729-746) calls `watch_live_command(executable=daemon_python, ...)` BEFORE `install_watch_autolaunch_script(..., dry_run=dry_run)`. `install_watch_autolaunch_script` gates all writes behind `if not dry_run` (watch_daemon.py lines 218-223), so in a `--dry-run` preview no file is written and no subprocess is launched — yet validation still fires and aborts. This is premature for dry-run previews of planned paths.

Test breakage confirmed empirically: `python -m pytest tests/test_watch_daemon.py` fails exactly four tests:
- test_watch_live_command_defaults_to_whole_screen (uses executable="/tmp/python")
- test_watch_live_command_supports_iterm_window_region_and_whole_screen (uses executable="python")
- test_install_watch_daemon_cli_dry_run (--dry-run --python /tmp/python -> "error: Executable path does not exist: /tmp/python", exit code 2)
- test_install_watch_daemon_cli_iterm_window_opt_in (--dry-run --python /tmp/python -> same error)

The $(...) sub-claim is also accurate: the deny-list at line 86 includes both `$` and backtick as individual characters, so `$(...)` command substitution is blocked via the `$` check and backtick substitution via the backtick check.

Severity: HIGH (not CRITICAL). It is a real regression that breaks the test suite and degrades the dry-run UX, but it is not a security/data-loss issue — the validation itself is a hardening measure; the bug is that it runs at the wrong place/time. The proposed fix (move validation into the non-dry-run install path, or skip filesystem checks in dry-run context, and update the four tests to use sys.executable or mock validate_executable) is correct and appropriate.

title: Cooldown revert discards debounce candidate state, restarting the stable-sample count
description: When a switch is produced by ModeSelector.observe() but blocked because the cooldown is active, run_watch_live reverts only selector.current_mode and selector._last_switch_luminance. It does NOT restore selector._candidate_mode / selector._candidate_count. On the successful-switch path, observe() already zeroed those candidate fields (watch.py:94-95). The net effect: after a cooldown-blocked switch the debounce counter is reset to 0, so the watcher must re-accumulate `stable` fresh consecutive samples in addition to waiting out the cooldown before it can switch again. Reproduced with stable=2: s1 (lum 0.80) -> candidate_count=1; s2 (lum 0.82) would switch but is cooldown-blocked -> candidate_count reset to 0; s3 (lum 0.83) restarts at candidate_count=1 (no switch); s4 (lum 0.84) finally switches. A user expecting the mode to apply as soon as the cooldown expires instead sees an extra (stable-1)-sample delay. The existing test (test_watch_live.py:153) only uses stable=1, which masks the bug entirely.
fix: Capture and restore the full debounce state around the cooldown revert. Either snapshot previous_candidate_mode = selector._candidate_mode and previous_candidate_count = selector._candidate_count before observe() and restore all four fields on revert, or add a public ModeSelector method (e.g. selector.snapshot()/selector.restore(snap)) that captures/restores current_mode, _last_switch_luminance, _candidate_mode and _candidate_count atomically. Add a regression test with stable>=2 that asserts the candidate count survives a cooldown-blocked switch.

verdict.reasoning:
The core mechanism is confirmed by reading the code. In src/term_chameleon/watch.py:91-96, the successful-switch path inside ModeSelector.observe() zeroes self._candidate_mode and self._candidate_count BEFORE returning switched=True. In src/term_chameleon/watch_live.py:160-165, when that switch is cooldown-blocked (now < next_allowed_switch), the revert restores ONLY selector.current_mode (line 161) and selector._last_switch_luminance (line 162); it does NOT restore _candidate_mode/_candidate_count. So after a cooldown-blocked switch the debounce counter is left at 0 and must re-accumulate `stable` fresh consecutive samples (in addition to waiting out the cooldown) before it can switch again. I reproduced this with a runnable script: with stable=2, a genuine cooldown-blocked second switch resets candidate_count to 0 and the post-cooldown re-switch requires re-accumulating samples. Default `stable=3` (config.py:19, watch_live.py:42), so the extra latency is up to (stable-1)=2 sample intervals. The existing test test_watch_live.py:153 uses stable=1, which makes the candidate counter irrelevant (a single sample always re-reaches threshold), masking the bug.

Important correction to the claim's specifics: the reported 4-sample reproduction (luminances 0.80/0.82/0.83/0.84, s2 cooldown-blocked) is WRONG. next_allowed_switch initializes to `start` (watch_live.py:142), so the FIRST switch is never cooldown-blocked — `now < next_allowed_switch` is false at s2. A cooldown block can only occur AFTER a prior successful switch has set next_allowed_switch = now + cooldown. Additionally, all four of those luminances classify to the same mode (bright-safe, lum>0.65), so they could never produce a second distinct switch anyway. The bug is real but only manifests on second-and-subsequent switches, and the proposed fix of capturing prev candidate state before observe() restores the pre-observe count (not the threshold-reached count), so the switch still lands one sample after cooldown expiry rather than instantly — the fix narrows but does not fully eliminate the described 'extra delay' framing.

Severity adjusted from HIGH to MEDIUM: this is a UX/latency debounce issue (mode applies a few sample-intervals later than ideal after cooldown), not a correctness bug, data loss, or security issue — the watcher still eventually switches to the correct mode. The claim's headline mechanism (candidate state discarded on cooldown revert) is accurate; its concrete reproduction steps are not.

title: ModeSelector private state mutated directly from watch_live, bypassing encapsulation
description: run_watch_live reads and writes ModeSelector private attributes (selector._last_switch_luminance at line 154, and reassigns selector.current_mode and selector._last_switch_luminance at lines 161-162) from outside the class. This is the mechanism behind the cooldown-revert bug above: because the revert logic lives outside ModeSelector and only knows about a subset of the selector's invariants, it cannot keep all internal counters consistent. Any future change to ModeSelector's internal fields (e.g. renaming _candidate_count) silently breaks the cooldown path with no type/contract checking.
fix: Move cooldown/revert handling into ModeSelector as a first-class operation, or expose explicit snapshot/restore methods so the full invariant is captured in one place. The caller should never touch _-prefixed fields.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the actual code. In /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/watch_live.py, run_watch_live reaches into ModeSelector's private state from outside the class:
- Line 154: previous_last_switch_luminance = selector._last_switch_luminance (reads the _-prefixed private field)
- Line 161: selector.current_mode = previous_mode (writes a public field)
- Line 162: selector._last_switch_luminance = previous_last_switch_luminance (writes the _-prefixed private field)

ModeSelector is defined in src/term_chameleon/watch.py (lines 60-97). Its observe() method, on a successful switch (lines 91-96), mutates FOUR pieces of state together: current_mode, _last_switch_luminance, _candidate_mode, and _candidate_count. The cooldown revert in watch_live only restores two of these (current_mode and _last_switch_luminance); it does not touch _candidate_mode/_candidate_count. This confirms the claim's central point: the revert logic lives outside the class and only knows a subset of the selector's invariants.

The encapsulation breach is real and accurately described. Python's _-prefix convention is being violated by the caller, and there is no type/contract checking — renaming _last_switch_luminance or _candidate_count would silently break the cooldown path (an assignment to a renamed attribute would just create a stray attribute rather than error). The proposed fix (move cooldown/revert into ModeSelector, or add explicit snapshot/restore methods) is idiomatic and sound.

Note this is a maintainability/encapsulation concern, not an active correctness bug: on the switched=True path the two unrestored fields (_candidate_mode, _candidate_count) were just zeroed by observe() at lines 94-95, and leaving them at 0/None is arguably the desired behavior after a cooldown rejection (candidate counting should restart fresh). So the current behavior is coincidentally consistent. The risk is forward-looking fragility, which matches a MEDIUM maintainability severity rather than HIGH. Keeping severity at MEDIUM as claimed.

title: Malformed/truncated PNG IHDR raises uncaught struct.error (not ValueError)
description: read_png passes the IHDR chunk payload directly to struct.unpack('>IIBBBBB', payload), which requires exactly 13 bytes. A PNG whose IHDR chunk is truncated (payload < 13 bytes) or padded (payload > 13 bytes) raises struct.error. struct.error is a subclass of Exception, NOT ValueError. The CLI top-level handler (cli.py:583) only catches (ValueError, OSError, json.JSONDecodeError, RuntimeError), so the program crashes with an unhandled traceback instead of a clean 'error: ...' message. Triggering input: any .png file beginning with the valid 8-byte signature followed by an IHDR chunk declaring a length other than 13 (e.g. a file truncated mid-IHDR). Verified: struct.unpack on a 5-byte payload raises struct.error and isinstance(e, ValueError) is False.
fix: Length-check the payload before unpacking: `if len(payload) < 13: raise ValueError('PNG IHDR chunk truncated')`, or wrap the unpack in try/except struct.error and re-raise as ValueError('malformed PNG IHDR').

verdict.reasoning:
Verified by reading the code and reproducing end-to-end.

CODE (src/term_chameleon/png.py:22-24): read_png passes the raw IHDR chunk payload straight into struct.unpack(">IIBBBBB", payload), which requires exactly 13 bytes, with no length pre-check. The chunk reader _chunks (lines 64-77) only validates that the declared chunk length fits within the buffer (raises ValueError on truncation past the buffer), but does NOT validate that IHDR's declared length equals 13. So a PNG with a valid 8-byte signature and an IHDR chunk whose declared length is, e.g., 5 reaches struct.unpack with a 5-byte payload.

EXCEPTION TYPE: Confirmed struct.error MRO is [error, Exception, BaseException, object] — it is NOT a subclass of ValueError, OSError, or RuntimeError. isinstance checks all returned False.

HANDLER (src/term_chameleon/cli.py:583): The only relevant try/except in the path catches (ValueError, OSError, json.JSONDecodeError, RuntimeError). struct.error matches none, so it propagates as an unhandled traceback instead of the clean "error: ..." message + exit code 2.

REACHABILITY: Confirmed full call chain from the CLI 'sample' and 'adapt-once' commands: _sample/_adapt_once -> decide_from_image/adapt_profile_from_image (adapt.py:40,72) -> analyze_image_file (screenshot_test.py:108) -> read_image_file routes .png suffix to read_png (screenshot_test.py:123-124). No intermediate try/except exists in png.py, screenshot_test.py, or adapt.py (grep found none), so nothing converts struct.error to ValueError before it reaches the top-level handler.

REPRODUCTION: Built a temp .png with valid signature + IHDR declaring length 5 + 5-byte payload; read_png raised struct.error with isinstance(ValueError)=False, isinstance(OSError)=False, isinstance(RuntimeError)=False.

The proposed fix (length-check payload before unpacking, or wrap unpack in try/except struct.error re-raising ValueError) is correct.

SEVERITY ADJUSTMENT: Downgraded HIGH -> MEDIUM. This is a robustness/UX defect (ugly uncaught traceback instead of a clean error message) triggered only by user-supplied malformed PNG input. No security, data-loss, or correctness-on-valid-input impact. Per the project's own severity rubric this fits MEDIUM (maintainability/robustness concern), though it is unambiguously a real bug.

title: Corrupt IDAT zlib stream raises uncaught zlib.error
description: zlib.decompress(b''.join(idat_parts)) is called with no error handling. A PNG with a valid signature/IHDR but corrupt or truncated IDAT compressed data raises zlib.error, which (like struct.error) is NOT a ValueError and is not caught by the CLI handler at cli.py:583. The exception propagates as an unhandled crash. Triggering input: a PNG file with a well-formed IHDR but IDAT payload bytes that are not a valid zlib stream. Verified: zlib.decompress(b'not valid zlib data here') raises zlib.error and isinstance(e, ValueError) is False.
fix: Wrap the decompress in try/except zlib.error and re-raise as ValueError('corrupt PNG image data'), so the CLI handler and other callers expecting ValueError behave consistently.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the code and reproducing end-to-end.

Code facts:
- /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/png.py:42 calls `raw = zlib.decompress(b"".join(idat_parts))` with no surrounding try/except.
- /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/cli.py:583 catches only `(ValueError, OSError, json.JSONDecodeError, RuntimeError)`.
- Verified `zlib.error.__mro__` is (zlib.error, Exception, BaseException, object) — it is NOT a subclass of ValueError, OSError, or RuntimeError, so the CLI handler does not catch it.

Reachability confirmed: CLI `sample` and `adapt-once` accept user `--image` paths (cli.py:373, 387) → `decide_from_image` / `adapt_profile_from_image` (adapt.py:40,72) → `analyze_image_file` (screenshot_test.py:124) → `read_png` (png.py:42). So an attacker/user-supplied PNG flows directly to the unguarded decompress.

Reproduction: Built a PNG with valid signature, valid 1x1 8-bit RGB IHDR, and IDAT payload `b'not valid zlib data here'`. `read_png` raised `zlib.error: Error -3 while decompressing data: incorrect header check`, and `isinstance(e, ValueError)` is False. This exception would propagate past the CLI handler as an uncaught traceback crash rather than the intended `error: ...` / exit code 2 path.

This is also inconsistent with the rest of png.py, which uniformly raises ValueError for every other corruption/format error (lines 16, 36, 38, 40, 46, 68, 75, 89, 99, 105, 128). Line 42 is the lone leak.

The claim's mechanism, triggering input, and propagation path are all accurate. The proposed fix (wrap in try/except zlib.error and re-raise as ValueError) is correct and consistent with existing code.

Severity adjusted from HIGH to MEDIUM: the impact is a crash/traceback on malformed input (poor error UX, not a graceful exit), not data loss, security compromise, or corruption. It only manifests in non-default CLI paths where a user explicitly points --image at a corrupt PNG. Real and worth fixing, but lower blast radius than HIGH implies.

title: Malformed iTerm profile color/transparency values raise uncaught TypeError
description: Color.from_iterm_dict calls float() on values pulled from arbitrary JSON. If a color component (Red/Green/Blue/Alpha Component) is a JSON array or object rather than a number, float() raises TypeError (NOT ValueError). The same applies to ItermProfile.transparency() and minimum_contrast() (iterm_profile.py:60,64) which do float(value) on untrusted JSON. TypeError is not in the CLI handler's caught set (cli.py:583), so commands like `doctor`, `fix`, `mode` crash with an unhandled traceback on a hand-edited or corrupted profile. Triggering input: a Dynamic Profile JSON with e.g. "Background Color": {"Red Component": [1,2], ...} or "Transparency": {"x":1}. Verified: Color.from_iterm_dict with a list component raises TypeError, isinstance(e, ValueError) is False; float({...}) raises TypeError.
fix: Validate types before conversion (e.g. `if not isinstance(value.get('Red Component'), (int,float)): raise ValueError(...)`), or catch TypeError alongside ValueError and re-raise as a ValueError with a clear message; alternatively add TypeError to the CLI handler's caught exceptions.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against source and empirically.

Code confirms claim:
- /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/color.py:32-38 — `Color.from_iterm_dict` does `float(value.get("Red Component", 0.0))` etc. on values from arbitrary JSON.
- /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/iterm_profile.py:58-64 — `minimum_contrast()` and `transparency()` do `float(value)` on untrusted JSON (`self.profile.get(...)`).
- /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/cli.py:399 `try:` wraps the command dispatch including doctor (400), fix (402), mode (445); the except at cli.py:583 catches only `(ValueError, OSError, json.JSONDecodeError, RuntimeError)` — TypeError is NOT caught.

Empirical confirmation (ran Python): `Color.from_iterm_dict({'Red Component': [1,2], ...})` raises TypeError with `isinstance(e, ValueError) == False`; a dict component likewise raises TypeError; `float({'x':1})` raises TypeError. By contrast `float('abc')` raises ValueError, so a bad string is handled but a JSON array/object is not.

Reachability confirmed: `_doctor` (cli.py:590) calls `diagnose(profile)`; diagnostics.py:117 calls `profile.transparency()` and :131 `profile.minimum_contrast()`, and cli.py:593-594 access `profile.background`/`profile.foreground` which call `Color.from_iterm_dict`. So a Dynamic Profile with e.g. `"Transparency": {"x":1}` or `"Background Color": {"Red Component": [1,2], ...}` makes doctor/fix/mode raise an uncaught TypeError, producing a stack trace instead of the intended `error: ...` / exit-2 path.

The bug is genuine. I downgrade HIGH->MEDIUM: impact is a robustness/error-handling defect (ugly traceback vs clean error message + exit 2) on a hand-edited or corrupted profile. No security exposure, no data loss, no crash of a long-running process. It is an input-validation gap at a system boundary that should be caught and re-raised as a ValueError or have TypeError added to the handler, which fits MEDIUM ("maintainability/robustness, consider fixing") rather than HIGH ("bug or significant quality issue blocking merge").

title: contrast_ratio ignores background alpha; transparent background treated as opaque
description: contrast_ratio blends the foreground over the background only when foreground.a < 1, but never blends the background and uses background.relative_luminance() directly. A background Color with a < 1 (transparent, common with iTerm 'Transparency' or RGBA PNG/profile data) has its alpha silently ignored: its luminance is computed as if fully opaque. Combined with blend_over always forcing result alpha = 1.0 (color.py:66), a translucent background produces a contrast ratio that does not reflect what is actually rendered, yielding misleading PASS/FAIL accessibility results. Verified: contrast_ratio(Color(1,1,1,0.5), Color(0,0,0,0.0)) returns 5.28 using bg luminance 0.0 as if opaque black.
fix: Either document and enforce that contrast inputs must be opaque (assert background.a == 1.0 / reject a<1 with a clear error), or composite the background over an assumed surface (e.g. the terminal/window color) before computing luminance so the ratio reflects the rendered pixels.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/contrast.py:6-14 and color.py:52-67. The code behaves exactly as claimed: contrast_ratio blends the foreground over the background only when foreground.a < 1 (line 8), but assigns bg = background directly (line 9) and never blends the background. relative_luminance() (color.py:52-58) uses only r,g,b and ignores self.a, so a background with a < 1 has its alpha silently treated as opaque. blend_over (color.py:60-67) hardcodes result alpha = 1.0 as stated.

The cited numerical example reproduces exactly: I ran contrast_ratio(Color(1,1,1,0.5), Color(0,0,0,0.0)) and got 5.280822809644651, with the transparent black background contributing relative_luminance() == 0.0 as if fully opaque.

Reachability is real: Color.from_iterm_dict (color.py:31-38) parses and retains "Alpha Component" < 1.0, and ItermProfile.background (iterm_profile.py:42-43) returns that Color. Backgrounds flow unblended into contrast_ratio at diagnostics.py:176, text_contrast.py:223, cli.py:599, and pixel_contrast.py:73. So a profile/background with alpha < 1 yields a contrast ratio that does not reflect rendered pixels, producing misleading WCAG PASS/FAIL results.

Severity remains MEDIUM rather than higher: the bug only manifests when a background Color carries an explicit alpha < 1, which is uncommon (iTerm's window "Transparency" is a separate profile key handled elsewhere, not the Background Color's Alpha Component). Notably visual.py:69 already composites effective_bg via blend_over before calling contrast_ratio, showing the authors knew backgrounds can need compositing, so the gap is the other call paths and the unconditional opaque-background assumption in contrast_ratio itself. The proposed fix (assert/reject a<1 backgrounds, or composite the background over an assumed surface) is sound.

title: iterm_connection.py: probe_iterm_connection() blocks indefinitely with no timeout
description: iterm2.run_until_complete(main) at line 26 has no timeout. If the iTerm2 daemon is unresponsive (e.g., running but stuck), run_until_complete blocks forever. The except blocks on lines 27-30 only catch SystemExit and generic Exception *after* the call returns — they cannot interrupt a hang. This freezes the CLI indefinitely when the user runs `term-chameleon status --live` or any live probe against a hung iTerm2 Python API.
fix: Wrap the iterm2.run_until_complete call in a threading.Thread with a timeout, or use asyncio.wait_for() inside the async main() to impose a deadline. Alternatively document that users must kill the process manually and add a --timeout CLI flag.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code at /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/iterm_connection.py:26. The code matches the claim exactly: `message = iterm2.run_until_complete(main)` is a synchronous blocking call with no timeout, and the `except SystemExit` / `except Exception` handlers at lines 27-30 only run after the call returns, so they cannot interrupt a hang in progress (this part of the claim is correct).

I traced the iterm2 package internals (installed at site-packages). `run_until_complete` -> `Connection.run` -> `loop.run_until_complete(async_connect(...))` -> `await coro(connection)`, which runs the probe's `async_get_app` RPC. The websocket is opened with `ping_interval=None` (keepalive pings disabled) and there is no `asyncio.wait_for`/deadline anywhere in the path. So if iTerm2 establishes the websocket but the daemon is stuck and never replies to the RPC (or the socket connect itself hangs), the call blocks indefinitely with no recovery. This freezes the calling thread.

Call sites confirmed: status.py:102 invokes it when `live=True` (i.e., `status --live`) and cli.py:1183 (`_iterm_connect_probe`) also calls it. So a hang would indeed freeze those CLI flows.

Severity adjusted from HIGH to MEDIUM. The "blocks forever" condition is real but narrow: the common failure modes fail fast, not hang. `ConnectionRefusedError` triggers `sys.exit(1)` in the package; daemon-absent / API-disabled / 401 / 406 paths raise or sys.exit. The indefinite hang requires the specific degraded state of an established connection with an unresponsive daemon, which is an edge case rather than routine usage. It is a genuine missing-timeout robustness gap (the proposed fix of asyncio.wait_for in main() or a threaded timeout is appropriate), but it does not freeze the CLI under normal "iTerm2 not available" conditions, so it is a maintainability/resilience concern rather than a high-severity routine break.

title: iterm_window.py:86 — int() conversion of AppleScript desktop bounds output has no error handling
description: Line 86: `parts = [int(part.strip()) for part in raw.split(',')]`. title: PID file treated as alive on PermissionError; corrupt PID indistinguishable from missing
description: pid_is_running returns True on PermissionError (assumes the process exists but is owned by another user). On systems with restrictive process-inspection policies this can report a dead/reused PID as running, causing the daemon to believe a watcher is active and refuse to start (or skip restart). Separately, read_pid returns None for both FileNotFoundError and ValueError, so a corrupted PID file (garbage contents) is silently treated the same as 'no daemon ever started', hiding state corruption. Combined with the documented read-then-check race (lines 180-186), a stale or corrupt .pid file can lead to duplicate or missing watcher processes.
fix: Distinguish the cases: log/raise on a corrupt (ValueError) PID file rather than collapsing to None, and reconsider returning True on PermissionError (validate the PID belongs to the expected process, e.g. by checking the command line, before assuming it is the watcher).

verdict.reasoning:
Code verified at src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py. The cited behavior is exactly as described: pid_is_running (lines 57-64) returns True on PermissionError; read_pid (lines 67-71) returns None for both FileNotFoundError and ValueError. The same logic is duplicated in the generated autolaunch script (_pid_is_running lines 170-177, _existing_watcher_running lines 180-185), which gates whether a new watcher starts (line 189). Both helpers also feed get_watch_daemon_status (lines 242, 251). So the factual description is accurate.

However, the severity is genuinely LOW, and parts lean toward NOT_A_BUG:

1. Returning True on PermissionError is the canonical, correct POSIX idiom for os.kill(pid, 0). EPERM means a process with that PID exists (owned by another user). psutil, supervisor, and CPython docs all treat EPERM as "alive". The PID file is written by the watcher itself (same user), so EPERM only arises on cross-user PID reuse, an extremely rare edge for a per-user ~/Library/Logs daemon. The "reports a dead/reused PID as running" scenario is real but near-zero probability. The proposed cmdline-validation fix adds notable complexity for a practically nonexistent case.

2. read_pid collapsing ValueError to None is real but benign: a corrupt PID file is treated as "no daemon", so the daemon starts a fresh watcher and overwrites the PID file (the desired recovery). The only loss is a log line on corruption, an observability nit, not a correctness defect.

3. The read-then-check TOCTOU (lines 180-189) is genuine but inherent to PID-file daemon coordination and is cited only as a contributing factor.

Net: the claim is technically real and accurately located, but it documents low-impact robustness/observability nits rather than a behavioral bug. LOW is the appropriate ceiling.

title: safe_io.py:39 — os.fsync() called unconditionally on all file descriptors, fails on special filesystems
description: os.fsync(handle.fileno()) at line 39 can raise OSError on filesystems that do not support fsync (e.g., NFS, certain tmpfs configurations, some network-mounted drives). This error is not caught, propagating as an unhandled OSError from atomic_write_text(). Every profile write, autolaunch script install, and pattern file write calls this path. The error message ('OSError: [Errno 22] Invalid argument') is not user-friendly and gives no context about which file was being written.
fix: Wrap the os.fsync() call in a try/except OSError and either ignore it (fsync is a best-effort durability hint, not correctness-critical for this use case) or re-raise with a friendlier message: 'could not fsync {target}: {exc}'.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed against the actual code. /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/safe_io.py line 39 is exactly `os.fsync(handle.fileno())`, called unconditionally inside `atomic_write_text()`. There is no try/except wrapping the fsync itself; the only surrounding handler (line 41 `except Exception:`) merely unlinks the temp file and re-raises, so any OSError from fsync propagates out of `atomic_write_text()` unhandled. I verified all callers (fixes.py:84, iterm_api.py:91, terminal_pattern.py:24, modes.py:38, install.py:50/108, watch_daemon.py:222) and none wrap the call in error handling, so the claim that profile writes, autolaunch installs, and pattern writes all hit this path is accurate.

The technical premise is also sound: os.fsync can raise OSError (commonly EINVAL) on file types/filesystems that do not support synchronization (some network mounts, sandboxes like gVisor, certain special/virtual filesystems). This is a well-known portability concern that mature projects (pip, etc.) guard against, and fsync here is a best-effort durability hint, not correctness-critical — the atomic rename (tmp.replace(target)) is what guarantees atomicity. So the proposed fix (suppress or re-raise with context) is reasonable.

However, I am downgrading severity from MEDIUM to LOW. On the platforms this tool actually targets (macOS/APFS via iTerm/Terminal, and standard Linux ext4/btrfs for Kitty/Ghostty/Alacritty), fsync works fine; the failure only manifests on uncommon/exotic filesystems. The impact is a non-user-friendly but accurate uncaught error, with no data loss (the temp file is cleaned up and the target is never partially written) and no security implication. It is a real robustness/portability defect worth a small guard, but its real-world likelihood and impact are low.

title: terminal_pattern.py:25 — chmod after atomic_write_text has no error handling
description: Line 25: `target.chmod(0o755)` after atomic_write_text() has no exception handling. On filesystems that do not support chmod (e.g., FAT32, some network mounts, or when the user lacks permission), this raises PermissionError or OSError. The file has already been written atomically, so it exists but is not executable. The same pattern appears in watch_daemon.py:223 and iterm_api.py:92. The caller gets a raw exception with no context about what failed or what the resulting state is.
fix: Wrap target.chmod(0o755) in a try/except OSError and raise a user-friendly error: 'script written to {target} but could not set executable bit: {exc}'. Return the target path regardless so callers know the file exists.

The same unguarded pattern genuinely exists at src/term_chameleon/iterm_api.py:92 and src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py:223 (the claim also missed a fourth instance at install.py:109). On filesystems that do not support chmod (FAT32, some network/SMB mounts) or when permissions are insufficient, chmod raises PermissionError/OSError, leaving the file written-but-not-executable and surfacing a raw exception to callers (e.g., cli.py:1304, live_stage.py:156, e2e_stage.py:37) with no context. So the factual claim is correct.\n\nHowever, the MEDIUM rating is too high. This is a local terminal/CLI dev tool writing scripts to local output/temp directories. The failure trigger is narrow: the writing user would have to run it against a chmod-unsupported mount, and atomic_write_text would have to succeed there first. The consequence is not data loss, corruption, or a security issue — it is an error-message-quality/UX gap. A raw OSError is still a reasonably actionable failure for a developer-facing tool, and the resulting state (non-executable script) fails loudly downstream rather than silently. The proposed fix (wrap in try/except OSError, raise a contextual message, still return target) is a reasonable robustness improvement, but the realistic impact places this at LOW rather than MEDIUM.

title: config.py:310 — _validate_preset passes None silently into PRESETS membership check
description: Lines 306-311: str_value(raw) can return None if raw is None, but the key-presence check at line 302 (`if key not in data`) means this path is only reached when the key exists. If a user sets `initial_mode = ` (empty string) in TOML, str_value returns '' (empty string), which is not in PRESETS, so an error is correctly appended. However, if raw is somehow None (not reachable via normal TOML parsing), `None not in PRESETS` is True, triggering an error message 'unknown preset/mode None' which is confusing. The code is correct in practice but relies on implicit None-not-in-PRESETS behavior without a guard.
fix: Add an explicit check after str_value: `if parsed is None: errors.append(...); return` to mirror the pattern in _validate_region (lines 322-325).

The code description is accurate:

However, the claim itself correctly concedes this is "correct in practice." For raw to be None, the parsed TOML dict would need a null value, which standard TOML (tomllib/tomli) cannot produce — there is no null type in TOML, and `initial_mode = ` with no value is a TOML syntax error, while `initial_mode = ""` yields '' not None. This is a genuine but purely cosmetic/consistency nit: a defensive guard is missing relative to the sibling function, but it has no functional impact because the triggering condition cannot occur via real TOML parsing. The proposed fix (mirroring the _validate_region None-guard) is reasonable for consistency. LOW severity is appropriate; this is a code-quality observation rather than a behavioral bug. isReal=true because the described code structure and the latent inconsistency genuinely exist as stated.

title: atomic_write_text cleanup swallows original exception when tmp.unlink() fails
description: In `atomic_write_text`, the `except Exception` block attempts to remove the temp file with `suppress(FileNotFoundError)`. If `tmp.unlink()` raises `OSError` or `PermissionError` (possible on network filesystems, or when the file descriptor is still open by another process), `suppress` does not catch those exceptions. They propagate out of the `with suppress(...)` block and replace the original exception before the bare `raise` executes. The caller then sees `PermissionError: [Errno 13] ...` from the cleanup attempt instead of the actual write failure (e.g. `OSError: [Errno 28] No space left on device`). Triggering input: call `atomic_write_text` on a path where writing fails and where the temp file's parent directory disallows unlinking (e.g. an immutable directory during testing).
fix:
Wrap the unlink in a broader `suppress(OSError)` (a superset of `FileNotFoundError`) so that cleanup failures never mask the original exception:

```python
except Exception:
    with suppress(OSError):
        tmp.unlink()
    raise
```

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code at /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/safe_io.py:41-44. The cited code matches exactly:

    except Exception:
        with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
            tmp.unlink()
        raise

The claim's mechanism is technically correct and I reproduced it empirically:
- FileNotFoundError IS a subclass of OSError, but PermissionError is NOT a subclass of FileNotFoundError. So suppress(FileNotFoundError) will NOT catch a PermissionError/OSError(other errno) raised by tmp.unlink().
- When tmp.unlink() raises such an error inside the except block, that new exception propagates out of the `with suppress(...)` block before the bare `raise` on line 44 ever runs. The caller then receives the cleanup exception (e.g. PermissionError [Errno 13]) rather than the original write failure (e.g. OSError [Errno 28] No space left on device).
- My reproduction confirmed: caller sees PermissionError, with the original OSError only preserved as __context__. A caller filtering by exception type/errno would be misled by the cleanup error.

The proposed fix (suppress(OSError), a superset of FileNotFoundError) correctly resolves it, since all unlink failures are OSError subclasses and would be suppressed, letting the original exception re-raise cleanly.

Severity adjustment: the bug is real but I downgrade HIGH -> LOW. The trigger requires a compound, uncommon condition — a write/replace failure AND a temp-file unlink that fails with something other than FileNotFoundError (network/immutable filesystems). It causes no data loss, corruption, or security impact; the worst outcome is a misleading exception type masking the true root cause (the original is still chained via __context__) plus a possibly-orphaned temp file. That is a diagnostics/error-handling quality concern, not a functional bug that breaks normal operation. Confidence is high on existence and mechanism; severity is the main thing I'm adjusting.

title: Non-atomic PID file write in generated autolaunch script enables duplicate-watcher race condition
description: The generated autolaunch script (the f-string template in `watch_autolaunch_script`) uses `PID_PATH.write_text(str(process.pid) + '\n')` (line 200 inside the template) after `subprocess.Popen`. This write is non-atomic. If the process is killed mid-write, the PID file is left truncated or empty. `read_pid()` then silently returns `None` (catches `ValueError`), so `_existing_watcher_running()` returns `False` on the next iTerm2 startup — triggering a second watcher process. Additionally, there is a TOCTOU race between `_existing_watcher_running()` (read PID, send signal 0) and the actual `Popen` + PID write: a second concurrent invocation (two iTerm2 windows opening simultaneously at startup) both see no running PID and both spawn watcher processes, resulting in two competing `watch-live` daemons that oscillate mode switches.
fix:
Use atomic PID file writing: write to a temp file in the same directory then `os.replace()`. Also add a lock file (e.g. `fcntl.flock`) around the check-and-spawn section to eliminate the TOCTOU window:

```python
import fcntl
lock_path = PID_PATH.with_suffix('.lock')
with open(lock_path, 'w') as _lock:
    fcntl.flock(_lock, fcntl.LOCK_EX)
    if _existing_watcher_running():
        return
    # ... Popen + atomic PID write ...
```

verdict.reasoning:
The cited code mechanics are accurate. In the generated autolaunch template (src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py:152-205), main() does _existing_watcher_running() (line 189) -> Popen (line 194) -> PID_PATH.write_text(str(process.pid) + "\n") (line 200). That write is non-atomic, and _existing_watcher_running() (line 180-185) plus read_pid() (line 67-71) both catch ValueError and treat a truncated/empty PID file as "not running". A check-then-spawn TOCTOU window does exist in code, and no lock file / flock exists anywhere in the module.

However, the claim's severity and triggering scenarios are substantially overstated:

1. TOCTOU trigger is unrealistic. The claim asserts "two iTerm2 windows opening simultaneously at startup" both invoke the script concurrently. That is factually wrong about iTerm2 AutoLaunch behavior: scripts in Library/Application Support/iTerm2/Scripts/AutoLaunch run once per application launch, not once per window. I confirmed (grep) the generated script (term_chameleon_watch_live.py) is only ever placed in that AutoLaunch dir and has no other invocation path in the codebase. So there is no normal concurrent-invocation path that would race the check-and-spawn.

2. Truncated-PID scenario is near-impossible and low-impact. The write is a single ~5-7 byte write_text. To leave it truncated/empty, the process must be SIGKILLed in the microsecond gap between open(truncate) and write of a handful of bytes. Even if it happened, the prior watcher was already dead (it was killed), so the next launch spawning one replacement watcher is correct behavior, not "two competing daemons oscillating."

3. The watcher mutates only the current session-local iTerm2 profile (per the template docstring and watch_live design), bounding blast radius.

So this is a genuine but marginal defensive-coding nit (non-atomic small write + a theoretical TOCTOU window with no realistic concurrent trigger), not a HIGH-severity duplicate-watcher race. The proposed os.replace() atomic write is a reasonable hardening; the flock suggestion guards a window that the actual deployment model (single per-launch invocation) does not exercise. Downgrading from HIGH to LOW. File: /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py:189-200.

title: ItermProfile.write() is non-atomic and can corrupt the profile on interruption
description: `ItermProfile.write()` calls `target.write_text(dumps_document(self.document), ...)` directly. This is a non-atomic write: if the process is killed or the disk fills up mid-write, `target` is left partially written and the iTerm2 Dynamic Profile JSON is corrupted. Every other write path in the codebase that mutates a profile file (`fix_file` in `fixes.py:84`, `apply_mode` in `modes.py:38`, `install_profile` in `install.py:50`) uses `atomic_write_text` with a rename-based commit. `ItermProfile.write()` is the sole exception. While it is not called anywhere in the current codebase (all callers were refactored to use `atomic_write_text` directly), the method exists as a public API and is a trap for future contributors.
fix:
Replace `write_text` with `atomic_write_text` from `safe_io`:

```python
from .safe_io import atomic_write_text

def write(self, path: Path | None = None) -> None:
    target = path or self.path
    if target is None:
        raise ValueError("no path supplied")
    atomic_write_text(target, dumps_document(self.document))
```

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the actual source. src/term_chameleon/iterm_profile.py:66-70 defines write() as: target = path or self.path; if target is None: raise ValueError; target.write_text(dumps_document(self.document), encoding="utf-8"). This is a direct, non-atomic write_text call with no temp file, no fsync, and no rename-based commit, so an interruption (kill, ENOSPC) can leave the target partially written and the Dynamic Profile JSON corrupted. This matches the claim.

The contrast is also accurate: safe_io.atomic_write_text uses mkstemp + write + flush + os.fsync + tmp.replace(target) with cleanup-on-error, i.e. a proper rename-based atomic commit. The three cited mutation paths all use it after backup_file: fixes.py uses atomic_write_text(p, dumps_document(profile.document)) (line ~84), modes.py uses atomic_write_text(target, dumps_document(profile.document)) (line ~38), install.py uses atomic_write_text(target, content) (line ~50). ItermProfile.write() is indeed the sole writer using raw write_text.

I also confirmed the "not called anywhere" claim: grep for callers of the write() method across src/ and tests/ returns no matches (the only .write( hits are handle.write inside atomic_write_text and unrelated module-level write_* functions). So the method is genuinely dead code, including no test coverage.

Conclusion: the defect is real and described correctly; the proposed fix (route through atomic_write_text) is correct. I lowered severity from MEDIUM to LOW because the method has zero callers and zero tests — there is no reachable code path that can actually corrupt a profile today. It is purely a latent consistency/maintainability trap for future contributors, which fits LOW more than MEDIUM. Note one minor wrinkle in the proposed patch: it writes dumps_document(self.document) but ignores backup_file, whereas every existing caller pairs atomic_write_text with backup_file first; that backup behavior lives in the callers, not the method, so the fix is still acceptable as written.

title: render_background_html CSS escaping only prevents </style> tag injection, not general CSS injection
description: `render_background_html` escapes the CSS background value only by replacing `</` with `<\/`, which prevents premature `</style>` tag termination. It does not sanitize other CSS-context-breaking characters such as `}` (which would close the surrounding ruleset), `url()` with a `javascript:` URI, or a bare `"` that would terminate the surrounding HTML attribute if the pattern is ever used in one. Currently `css_background` is always a hardcoded value from `BACKGROUND_CSS`, so no live injection path exists. However, `render_background_html` is a public function with a `css_background: str` parameter, and the docstring does not warn callers that arbitrary CSS is unsafe.
fix:
If the function is intended to accept only the hardcoded `BACKGROUND_CSS` values, validate the input against that allowlist:

```python
if css_background not in BACKGROUND_CSS.values():
    raise ValueError(f"css_background must be a known BACKGROUND_CSS value")
```

If the function must remain general, document clearly that `css_background` must be trusted/sanitized by the caller, and at minimum also escape `}` and `"` for defensive depth.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/background_html.py:25. The escaping is exactly `css_background.replace("</", "<\\/")`, which only prevents premature `</style>` tag termination. It does NOT sanitize `}` (which would break out of the `body {...}` ruleset at lines 39-41), `url(javascript:...)`, or `"`. The function `render_background_html(name, css_background)` (line 23) is public, accepts an arbitrary `str`, has no docstring/warning, and performs no allowlist validation. The claim is accurate that this is incomplete CSS sanitization.\n\nHowever, the claim itself correctly states there is NO live injection path: I confirmed via grep that the only caller is `write_background_html` (line 68), which feeds only the hardcoded `BACKGROUND_CSS` dict values (lines 8-14). The test also passes a literal constant. No user-controlled or external input ever reaches `css_background`. So this is a latent defensive-depth / documentation-hygiene issue, not an exploitable vulnerability. LOW severity is correct and not overstated — the claim is honest about the lack of exploit path. The proposed fixes (allowlist validation against BACKGROUND_CSS.values(), or a docstring warning the caller to pass only trusted CSS, plus escaping `}` and `\"`) are reasonable hardening.

title: _validate_preset silently reports 'unknown preset None' instead of 'expected string' when config value is null
description: When data[key] is a TOML null (Python None), str_value(raw) returns None (verified). Standard TOML has no null literal — there is no way to write a key whose value is `None`. So "disk full / missing file / malformed JSON" between the write and the read does not represent a realistic, triggerable failure path.

2. The second sub-claim is factually incorrect. `not item.get("passed")` does NOT "silently pass" when an element is not a dict — calling .get on a non-dict (e.g. str/int) raises AttributeError. Moreover the data is always a list of dicts (json.dumps([asdict(c) | {"passed": ...} for c in checks])), so a non-dict element never occurs. The proposed isinstance guard addresses a non-existent condition.

Net: this is a legitimate but minor defensive-hardening nicety (wrapping the read in try/except is good practice), not a MEDIUM-severity bug. The crash is not realistically triggerable given the synchronous same-process write immediately prior, and the "silently passes" claim is wrong. Adjusted to LOW.

title: CLI unreachable code after try/except at lines 586-587

title: watch_live.py:154,162 directly mutates private ModeSelector state outside the class
description: The cooldown enforcement code at lines 154-164 reads and writes `selector._last_switch_luminance` (a name-mangled private attribute) and `selector.current_mode` directly from the run_watch_live() loop. title: safe_io.atomic_write_text silently loses OSError from tmp.unlink() in exception handler
description: The exception handler at lines 41-44 uses `suppress(FileNotFoundError)` around tmp.unlink(). If unlink raises OSError or PermissionError (e.g., the temp file is locked on NFS), the suppress block does NOT suppress it — suppress(FileNotFoundError) only suppresses FileNotFoundError. This would cause a secondary exception to be raised inside an already-active except block, replacing the original exception context with the OSError from unlink. The user sees a permission error on cleanup instead of the original write error.
fix: Change `suppress(FileNotFoundError)` to `suppress(OSError)` to safely suppress all cleanup failures, or catch OSError explicitly and log it before re-raising the original exception.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading /Users/bodhi/Developer/term-chameleon/src/term_chameleon/safe_io.py:41-44. The code is exactly: `except Exception: / with suppress(FileNotFoundError): tmp.unlink() / raise`.

The Python semantics in the claim are accurate: contextlib.suppress(FileNotFoundError) only suppresses FileNotFoundError. If tmp.unlink() raises a different OSError subclass (e.g., PermissionError), it is NOT suppressed and propagates out of the `with` block. Since this occurs inside an active `except` handler, the cleanup exception becomes the propagating exception and the subsequent `raise` (which would re-raise the original write error) never executes. The user-facing primary exception thus becomes the unlink error rather than the original write failure.

Two corrections to the claim's framing that bear on severity:
1. The original exception is not silently "lost" — Python chains it via __context__, so it still appears in the traceback ("During handling of the above exception..."). It is demoted from the primary message, not erased.
2. The triggering scenario is narrow: tmp is a file mkstemp just created in target.parent, owned by the same process. A non-FileNotFoundError OSError on unlinking your own fresh temp file requires unusual conditions (NFS quirks, directory permission changes, Windows locking). This is plausible but uncommon.

Impact is confined to a confusing error message and a possibly-leftover temp file under rare conditions — no data corruption, no incorrect successful write. The proposed fix (suppress(OSError)) is reasonable since the existing code already deems unlink failures non-fatal. This is a real but genuinely minor code-quality issue; LOW severity is correct.

title: PNG reader: struct.unpack raises struct.error (not ValueError) on truncated IHDR before validation can run
description: At line 22, `struct.unpack('>IIBBBBB', payload)` is called on the raw IHDR payload bytes before any length check. This format requires exactly 13 bytes. If the IHDR chunk is shorter (e.g., a fuzz input with a corrupt length field), struct.unpack raises struct.error: 'unpack requires a buffer of 13 bytes'. The validation check at lines 29-35 that raises ValueError('PNG missing IHDR') cannot run because the exception happens first. Callers expecting only ValueError from read_png() would get an unhandled struct.error.
fix: Before struct.unpack, add: `if len(payload) < 13: raise ValueError('IHDR chunk too short')`

At lines 22-24, struct.unpack(">IIBBBBB", payload) is called directly on the IHDR payload before any length validation. The format requires exactly 13 bytes. The _chunks generator (lines 64-77) sizes each payload from the attacker-controlled length field (data[start:end] where end = start + length) with no check that an IHDR chunk is 13 bytes, so a crafted PNG with a short IHDR length yields a sub-13-byte payload. I confirmed via Python that struct.unpack(">IIBBBBB", b"\x00\x00\x00\x05\x08") raises struct.error: "unpack requires a buffer of 13 bytes", and that struct.error is NOT a subclass of ValueError (MRO: struct.error -> Exception -> BaseException). Therefore the ValueError("PNG missing IHDR") at line 36 never runs, and a caller catching only ValueError (a reasonable assumption since every other failure path in read_png raises ValueError) would receive an unhandled struct.error. The claim is accurate on every cited point. Severity is correctly LOW: untrusted file input at a system boundary, but the only consequence is an inconsistent exception type, not a crash beyond an exception, memory issue, or security flaw. Minor note: the proposed fix (len(payload) < 13) is incomplete because struct.unpack also rejects payloads LONGER than 13 bytes, so an exact-equality check or try/except would be more robust, but this does not affect the validity of the reported issue.

title: live_stage._escape_applescript_string error message references shlex.quote which is irrelevant to AppleScript
description: The error message at line 102 (which also triggers the E501 ruff lint error at 102:101) says: 'AppleScript strings cannot contain newlines; use shlex.quote() on shell commands'. shlex.quote() is a shell escaping function, not an AppleScript escaping function.
